description: Fix three friction points discovered through live testing of the plugin development workflow: (1) configSchema example in plugin-class.mdx is missing 'override readonly' which is required by the scaffolded tsconfig's noImplicitOverride setting, causing TS4114 compilation errors; (2) getConfig() — the only way to read user-configured settings in tool handlers — is not documented in the utilities reference; (3) the streaming-progress.mdx example imports ToolHandlerContext but doesn't use it, failing biome lint with noUnusedImports.
qualityChecks: cd docs && npm run build && npm run type-check && npm run lint && npm run knip && npm run format:check

userStories.title: Fix configSchema example in plugin-class.mdx to use override readonly
userStories.description: The configSchema property example in plugin-class.mdx shows 'readonly configSchema: ConfigSchema = {...}' without the 'override' modifier. Since the scaffolded tsconfig.json enables noImplicitOverride:true, following this example exactly produces TS4114: 'This member must have an override modifier because it overrides a member in the base class OpenTabsPlugin'. Add 'override' to both the example declaration and the surrounding explanation text.
acceptanceCriteria: The configSchema code example in plugin-class.mdx uses 'override readonly configSchema: ConfigSchema' not 'readonly configSchema: ConfigSchema'
The surrounding text mentions that override is required because the scaffolded tsconfig enables noImplicitOverride
userStories.notes:
Files:
- docs/content/docs/sdk/plugin-class.mdx (fix configSchema example)

Context:
The configSchema example at docs/content/docs/sdk/plugin-class.mdx line 132-143:

```typescript
**Example — declaring a configSchema:**

```typescript
import type { ConfigSchema } from '@opentabs-dev/plugin-sdk';

class MyPlugin extends OpenTabsPlugin {
  readonly configSchema: ConfigSchema = {
    instanceUrl: { type: 'url', label: 'Instance URL', required: true },
  };
  // ...
}
```

The base class OpenTabsPlugin declares configSchema as:
```typescript
readonly configSchema?: ConfigSchema;
```
(in platform/plugin-sdk/src/index.ts line ~102)

With noImplicitOverride:true in the scaffolded tsconfig.json, TypeScript requires 'override' when a subclass declares a property that exists on the base class.

Changes:
1. Change `readonly configSchema: ConfigSchema = {` to `override readonly configSchema: ConfigSchema = {` in the code example
2. Add a note near the example: something like 'The override modifier is required because the scaffolded tsconfig.json enables noImplicitOverride — omitting it causes TS4114.'

Constraints:
- lifecycle-hooks.mdx already explains noImplicitOverride for lifecycle hook methods — this fix adds the same clarification for configSchema
- Do NOT change any other property examples (urlPatterns, homepage, etc.) — configSchema is the special case here because it's optional on the base class
- The fix should be consistent with how lifecycle-hooks.mdx explains override

userStories.title: Add getConfig() documentation to utilities.mdx
userStories.description: getConfig(key) is the only way to read user-configured settings (declared in configSchema) from inside tool handlers. It is documented in plugin-sdk/CLAUDE.md but does not appear anywhere in the docs site — not in utilities.mdx, not in plugin-class.mdx, not in plugin-development.mdx. A developer who declares configSchema will not know how to access the values without reading SDK source code. Add a 'Plugin Settings' or 'Config Utilities' section to utilities.mdx documenting getConfig.
acceptanceCriteria: utilities.mdx contains a section documenting getConfig(key)
The section shows the function signature: function getConfig(key: string): string | number | boolean | undefined
The section includes a usage example showing import and usage in a tool handler
getConfig is added to the top-level import list at the start of utilities.mdx
userStories.notes:
Files:
- docs/content/docs/sdk/utilities.mdx (add getConfig section and update import list)

Context:
The utilities.mdx file has sections: DOM Utilities, Fetch Utilities, Storage Utilities, Page State Utilities, Timing Utilities, Logging, Readiness.

The file ends at line 1022 with the Readiness / notifyReadinessChanged section.

The top-level import list at lines 9-22:
```typescript
import {
  waitForSelector, waitForSelectorRemoval, querySelectorAll, getTextContent, getMetaContent, observeDOM,
  fetchFromPage, fetchJSON, postJSON, postForm, postFormData, putJSON, patchJSON, deleteJSON,
  fetchText, buildQueryString, httpStatusToToolError, parseRetryAfterMs, parseRateLimitHeader, stripUndefined,
  getLocalStorage, setLocalStorage, removeLocalStorage,
  getSessionStorage, setSessionStorage, removeSessionStorage,
  getCookie,
  getAuthCache, setAuthCache, clearAuthCache, findLocalStorageEntry,
  getPageGlobal, getCurrentUrl, getPageTitle,
  retry, sleep, waitUntil,
  log,
  notifyReadinessChanged,
} from '@opentabs-dev/plugin-sdk';
```

The actual getConfig implementation from platform/plugin-sdk/src/config.ts:
```typescript
export const getConfig = (key: string): string | number | boolean | undefined => {
  const config = (globalThis as Record<string, unknown>).__openTabs?.pluginConfig as
    | Record<string, string | number | boolean>
    | undefined;
  return config?.[key];
};
```

Changes:
1. Add getConfig to the import list (after notifyReadinessChanged or as its own line)
2. Add a new section before the Readiness section (or after Storage Utilities — place it wherever makes logical sense given it's about reading config, not DOM/fetch/storage/timing) titled '## Config Utilities' or '## Plugin Settings'
3. Document getConfig(key):
   - Signature: `function getConfig(key: string): string | number | boolean | undefined`
   - Description: Reads a resolved setting value from the plugin's configuration. Values are injected into the page context before the adapter IIFE runs.
   - Return value: The setting value as string, number, or boolean; undefined if the key is not configured.
   - Usage example showing a tool handler reading instanceUrl:

```typescript
import { getConfig } from '@opentabs-dev/plugin-sdk';

// In a tool handler:
const instanceUrl = getConfig('instanceUrl') as string | undefined;
if (!instanceUrl) {
  throw ToolError.validation('Plugin not configured — set instanceUrl in OpenTabs settings');
}
const data = await fetchJSON(`${instanceUrl}/api/items`);
```

Constraints:
- This section should cross-reference plugin-class.mdx for how to declare configSchema
- Do NOT add getConfig to the main plugin-development.mdx guide — that guide is already long enough
- The section placement should be after the Storage Utilities section since reading config is conceptually related to reading settings/storage

userStories.title: Fix unused ToolHandlerContext import in streaming-progress.mdx example
userStories.description: The primary code example in streaming-progress.mdx imports 'import type { ToolHandlerContext } from @opentabs-dev/plugin-sdk' but does not use it in a type annotation. The handle function's context parameter gets its type inferred from defineTool's generic signature — no explicit annotation is needed. This unused import fails biome lint with noUnusedImports. Remove the import, or add an explicit context type annotation to make the import used.
acceptanceCriteria: The streaming-progress.mdx code example does not have an unused ToolHandlerContext import
If ToolHandlerContext import is kept, it must be used as an explicit type annotation on the context parameter
userStories.notes:
Files:
- docs/content/docs/guides/streaming-progress.mdx

Context:
The code example at lines 1-2 of the example block:
```typescript
import { defineTool, fetchJSON, ToolError } from '@opentabs-dev/plugin-sdk';
import type { ToolHandlerContext } from '@opentabs-dev/plugin-sdk';
import { z } from 'zod';
```

The handle function at line 40:
```typescript
handle: async (params, context) => {
```

The defineTool type signature provides `context?: ToolHandlerContext` through generics, so the import is unused for inference purposes. biome lint/correctness/noUnusedImports flags this.

Changes (two options — pick whichever reads more clearly for the docs):
Option A (simpler): Remove the unused import. The context parameter is still typed through inference.
Option B (more explicit/educational): Keep the import but add it as an explicit type annotation:
```typescript
handle: async (params, context?: ToolHandlerContext) => {
```

Recommend Option A (remove the import) because:
1. It's the minimal fix
2. The inferred type is correct and sufficient
3. Having the import creates confusion about whether it's needed

Constraints:
- Only change the ToolHandlerContext import line in the code example — do not change any other part of the example
- The context?.reportProgress() calls throughout the handle function must remain unchanged
